rows: Does the law BAN cross-border transfer OR require data to be PROCESSED domestically?

{"1": "Covers personal data OR applies horizontally; ALSO 1 if two or more such requirements on non-personal/specific data (or targeting more than one economy).", "0.5": "A single requirement on non-personal/specific data or one economy only.", "0": "Transfer free of such requirements."}

["Transfer allowed if conditions are met -> that is P6-I4 (conditional flow), NOT a ban.", "Banking/professional secrecy or confidentiality duties are NOT transfer bans.", "A copy-stored-domestically rule without a transfer ban -> P6-I2.", "Transfer/amalgamation of a BUSINESS, undertaking, assets or shares (e.g. bank business-transfer schemes) is NOT a data transfer — Pillar 6 concerns DATA moving across borders."]
Must a COPY of the data be stored domestically (transfer may still be allowed)?

{"1": "Covers personal data OR applies horizontally; ALSO 1 if two or more such requirements on non-personal/specific data.", "0.5": "A single non-personal/specific-data storage rule.", "0": "No local-storage requirement."}
["Local storage does NOT itself forbid transfer -> not P6-I1.", "A local server/data-centre requirement to OPERATE -> P6-I3, not storage."]
Are local servers / data centres / facilities required AS A CONDITION of providing the service?

{"1": "Any infrastructure requirement exists (local data centre/server as a PRECONDITION of service, or transfer conditioned on infrastructure).", "0": "None. Rules governing already-established data centres are recorded with score 0."}

["'Must establish network access control POLICIES' is a rulebook, NOT infrastructure (5-Jun trap).", "Encryption / network segmentation / crypto-key rules -> P7-I2 cybersecurity, NOT 6.3.", "Storage of a copy on rented capacity -> P6-I2; 6.3 needs a facility precondition.", "Rules on ALREADY-ESTABLISHED data centres (security controls, registration) -> RECORD but score 0.00 (internal guide, 4-Jul read).", "Data-centre LICENSING regimes -> indicator 9.4, NOT 6.3."]
Is transfer permitted IF conditions are met (consent / adequacy / contractual safeguards / approval)?
{"1": "Conditions cover personal data (any coverage) OR apply horizontally (even non-personal data).", "0.5": "Non-personal/specific data, or sectoral only.", "0": "No conditions on transfer."}

["If NO transfer path exists at all -> P6-I1 ban, not 6.4.", "If transfer stays possible once conditions are met, it is NOT a ban (never code as 6.1).", "The provision must concern DATA/personal data moving ACROSS BORDERS. Transfer of a BUSINESS/undertaking/assets/shares (e.g. Banking Act business-transfer schemes, company amalgamations) is NOT a data-transfer condition.", "Disclosure of information to a DOMESTIC authority (tax, regulator, court) is not a cross-border data-flow condition (government access may be P7-I5)."]
Not in agreement with binding commitments on data transfer
Has the economy joined NO agreement with binding cross-border data-transfer commitments?
{"1": "No binding data-transfer agreement.", "0": "At least one binding agreement."}
Lack of comprehensive legal framework for data protection
Does the economy LACK a comprehensive (horizontal) personal-data-protection law?

{"1": "No comprehensive framework exists.", "0.5": "Sectoral-only laws; OR horizontal-but-thin (e.g. missing right-to-rectification — \"not comprehensive enough\", Juntong 5 Jun). Organizers invite going deeper than the binary here.", "0": "A comprehensive horizontal framework exists."}
["Sectoral DP laws are RECORDED but do not make the framework comprehensive.", "A cybersecurity law does not answer the data-protection question -> P7-I2."]
Lack of dedicated legal framework for cybersecurity
Does the economy LACK a law specifically designed for cybersecurity (network security, incident response, critical infrastructure, cyber-risk)?

{"1": "No cybersecurity framework at all.", "0.5": "A NON-DEDICATED framework (relies on other laws for incident monitoring/detection/prevention/mitigation) and/or a dedicated but SECTORAL-ONLY framework.", "0": "A dedicated HORIZONTAL cybersecurity framework exists."}

["Scattered security clauses inside a privacy/sectoral law do NOT make a dedicated framework.", "Sectoral notices (e.g. MAS Cyber Hygiene for financial sector) are recorded but are NOT the controlling evidence when a horizontal act exists (Assignment-1 trap; the MAS 2019 notice was also superseded by FSM-N16)."]
Minimum period of data retention requirements
Must data be kept FOR AT LEAST a specified period (days/months/years)?

{"1": "A minimum retention period is clearly specified (keep >= X days/months/years).", "0": "No minimum period specified. A retention requirement WITHOUT a specified minimum period is still RECORDED, scored 0 (4-Jul internal-guide rule)."}
["'Do NOT keep longer than necessary' is a retention LIMIT (data minimisation) — the OPPOSITE. NEVER 7.3 (their most-repeated warning)."]
["Sectoral law: anti-money-laundering, tax, companies/accounting, telecom, health, employment."]
Must organisations appoint a Data Protection Officer and/or run Data Protection Impact Assessments?

{"1": "DPO (or DPO + DPIA) required across ALL sectors.", "0.5": "Sector-specific DPO/DPIA requirement only.", "0": "Neither required. (Guide-only theoretical tier: DPIA-only = 0.25 — never yet observed in the data.)"}
["Having a privacy law in general is NOT enough — the question is the specific accountability mechanisms."]
Requirements to allow government access to personal data
Can the government access personal data WITHOUT authorization of an independent judicial body (court decision / warrant / equivalent)?

THE COURT-ORDER TEST (4-Jul correction — the score-deciding question): access that does NOT require independent judicial authorization scores 1; a power exercisable ONLY under a court order/warrant supports score 0. Record both kinds of provision, but only warrantless access drives the score.
{"1": "A measure allows government access WITHOUT independent judicial authorization.", "0": "No such measure — including where every access power requires a court order/warrant."}

["Exceptions inside the privacy act often point to the REAL access powers defined elsewhere — follow them.", "Access gated on a court order / judicial warrant does NOT score 1 — it supports score 0 (record it, note the judicial safeguard)."]
["Look BEYOND the privacy statute: criminal procedure codes, surveillance/lawful-access law, telecom law, national security law (their explicit research lesson — and our richest NEW-evidence vein)."]

Personal Data Protection Act 2012;

Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020
Singapore does not implement ban on data transfer and local processing requirement.

According to Section 199, every company must retain accounting for a period of 5 years from the end of the financial year in which the transactions or operations to which those records relate are completed. This data shall be kept at the registered office of the company or other places in Singapore specified by the director. This provision is to ensure that the company will have sufficient data to enable them to properly audited and true financial statements.

Specifically, accounting records under this Act refers to records in relation to a corporation, includes such working papers and other documents as are necessary to explain the methods and calculations by which accounts of the corporation are made up (Section 4).

Personal Data Protection Act 2012;

Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020
Singapore does not implement infrastructure requirement.

Personal Data Protection Act 2012;

Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020

According to Section 26, an organisation may only transfer personal data outside Singapore if it has taken appropriate steps to ensure that: 
- it will comply with the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) obligations in respect of the transferred personal data while it remains in its possession or under its control; and 
- the recipient outside of Singapore is bound by legally enforceable obligations to provide a standard of protection to the personal data transferred that is comparable to that under the PDPA. 

In this regard, “legally enforceable obligations” would include obligations imposed on the recipient: (i) under law (e.g. the recipient’s relevant national data privacy legislation); (ii) under any contract: (a) requiring the recipient to provide a standard of protection to the personal data transferred that is at least comparable to the protection under the PDPA; and (b) specifying the countries and territories to which the personal data may be transferred under the contract; (iii) under binding corporate rules (see below); or (iv) any other legally binding instrument.

An organisation will be taken to have satisfied the second requirement if the individual consents to the transfer of the personal data to the recipient in that country.

Personal Data Protection Act 2012;

Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020

The Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA) provides a baseline standard of protection for personal data in Singapore. It complements sector-specific legislative and regulatory frameworks such as the Banking Act and Insurance Act, Healthcare Services Act, Telecommunications Act, etc. The PDPA comprises various requirements governing the collection, use, disclosure and care of personal data in Singapore. Personal Data Protection Commission (PDPC) administers and enforces the PDPA.

Moreover, the PDPA amendment in 2021 introduces a number of key reforms, including mandatory data breach notification requirements, amendments to the consent obligation, offences for egregious mishandling of personal data, prohibitions relating to the use of dictionary attacks and address-harvesting software, and the PDPC's power to accept voluntary undertakings as part of its enforcement regime.

Under Section 47 of the Banking Act 1970 (s47 BA), banks and their officers are responsible for safeguarding the confidentiality of customer information. A bank or any of its officers may not disclose customer information to any other person, except where specified under the Third Schedule, e.g. where the bank has obtained written consent from the customer to disclose the information. 
Section 47 BA also stipulates penalties for breaching these confidentiality requirements.

Cybersecurity Act 2018 establishes a legal framework for the oversight and maintenance of national cybersecurity in Singapore. The key objectives of the Cybersecurity Act are to: (i) strengthen the protection of Critical Information Infrastructure against cyber-attacks; (ii) authorize the government to prevent and respond to cybersecurity threats and incidents; (iii); establish a framework for sharing cybersecurity information, and (iv) establish a licensing framework for cybersecurity service providers. Amendments to the Act were made in May 2024 to update the Act so that it keeps pace with the developments in the cyber threat landscape, as well as our evolving technological operating context. The key amendments: (i) updated existing provisions relating to cybersecurity of Critical Information Infrastructure, and (ii) expanded CSA’s oversight to cover new classes of regulated entities.

Personal Data Protection Act 2012;

Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2020

Section 25 of the PDPA requires an organisation to cease retaining its documents containing personal data, or remove the means by which the personal data can be associated with particular individuals, as soon as it is reasonable to assume that the purpose for which that personal data was collected is no longer being served by retention of the personal data, and retention is no longer necessary for legal or business purposes. This obligation to cease the retention of personal data is referred to in these Guidelines as the Retention Limitation Obligation.

The telecom licensee shall maintain the following data records for 12 calendar months, which shall be made available for inspection by authorised Singapore government agencies: assigned source IP address and date and time stamps; and assigned user ID/user name (e.g., subscriber records).

According to Section 199, every company must retain accounting for a period of 5 years from the end of the financial year in which the transactions or operations to which those records relate are completed. This data shall be kept at the registered office of the company or other places in Singapore specified by the director. This provision is to ensure that the company will have sufficient data to enable them to properly audited and true financial statements.

Specifically, accounting records under this Act refers to records in relation to a corporation, includes such working papers and other documents as are necessary to explain the methods and calculations by which accounts of the corporation are made up (Section 4).
Section 67: Businesses are required to keep records and accounts for a period of 5 years from the Year of Assessment to which any income relates.
Section 95: (For current employees) Employers must keep detailed employment records for latest two years. (For ex-employees) Records must be kept for one year after the employee leaves employment.

Under Section 11(3), each organisation is required to appoint one or more data protection officers to be responsible to ensure the organisation’s compliance with the Personal Data Protection Act. Moreover, data protection impact assessment (“DPIA”) guides has been implemented. 

Organisations are also advised to conduct data protection impact assessments before releasing products or services that are likely to be accessed by children in order to meet their Accountability Obligation under the PDPA.

Under the Criminal Procedure Code, (section 39 and 40) police officers have the power to access computers and the power to access decrypted information. Moreover, (section 20) empowers the police to order the "production of any document or other thing" for the purposes of criminal investigations. Any person who obstructs the lawful exercise by a police officer or an authorised person shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $10,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or to both.

The Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 (PDPA) prohibits the transfer of personal data out of Malaysia unless such transfer is to a country, which has been specified and recorded in the Official Gazette by the Minister. Currently, no countries have been specified officially. Notwithstanding the prohibition on transfers of personal data out of the country, the PDPA sets out a number of exceptions to the prohibition, such as where the consent of the data subject has been obtained for such transfer and where the transfer is necessary for the performance of a contract between the parties. When in doubt as to whether the exemptions on data transfer apply, the prudent approach would be to obtain consent from the data subject in respect of such out of Malaysia transfer. In relation to outsourcing, a data user is not allowed to share data with third parties unless the consent of the individual has been obtained.

The PDPA applies broadly across sectors to regulate the processing of personal data for commercial transactions. Any personal data processed in Malaysia must adhere to PDPA standards, including specific security and privacy principles. Section 6 requires that personal data should not be processed unless the data subject consents, or it is necessary for specific legal reasons. The Security Principle in Section 9 enforces practical steps to protect personal data from unauthorized or accidental access during processing, which aligns with local data storage requirements. Section 129 of the PDPA mentions restrictions on transferring personal data to places outside Malaysia unless the destination has data protection laws or the transfer meets specific conditions.

The Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector 2017 mandates that personal data be processed according to PDPA principles. Specific rules about data transfer abroad are mentioned in the document, limiting cross-border data transfers without compliance. Similar requirements for the Communications Sector are set out in the Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, especially for network and service providers. Both banking and communications sectors mention compliance with the Data Transfer Abroad clause, ensuring that any cross-border data transfer adheres to Malaysia’s data protection standards duscussed in other relevant subpillars.

Malaysia currently does not have a strict data localization law that mandates all personal data to be stored exclusively within the country. However, the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 includes provisions that regulate cross-border data transfers, requiring that personal data can only be transferred out of Malaysia if the recipient country provides an adequate level of data protection. This means that while data can be stored and processed abroad, certain safeguards must be in place to protect personal data.

For certain sectors, like banking and finance, there are sector-specific guidelines (e.g., the Banking and Financial Sector Code of Practice) that impose more stringent conditions on data transfers and suggest that local processing is preferable to ensure compliance with security and privacy standards. These requirements do not constitute a blanket local storage mandate but make local storage a practical solution for ensuring compliance with security obligations, especially for sensitive data.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017

In the Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector (2017), relevant local processing requirements are outlined underSection 3.1.1. Data users (banks and financial institutions) must process (e.g., to collect, use, modify, store and/or dispose of) personal data only with consent from the data subject, except in cases of legal necessity. Banks must take practical steps to secure personal data, which includes storing data locally in secure systems and preventing unauthorized access or loss (Security Principle Section 3.1.4).

Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010;

Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

The Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) provides rules for handling personal data in the communications sector:
- Local Processing Requirements:
	• General Principle (Section 3.1): Personal data must be processed within Malaysia with proper consent from data subjects unless otherwise legally required.
	• Security Principle (Section 3.4): Communications licensees must ensure that secure systems are in place for storing and processing personal data, which includes local data storage and restricting unauthorized access.

Section 24 of the Service Tax Act 2018 (Act 807) sets out the obligations for recordkeeping by taxable persons. Every taxable person must keep complete, accurate, and up-to-date records of all transactions that affect or may affect their liability to service tax. This includes records of all taxable services provided or received (such as invoices, receipts, debit and credit notes), records of imported taxable services, and any other records as may be determined by the Director General. These records must be preserved for seven years from the most recent date to which they relate, must be kept in either the national language or English, and must be retained in Malaysia unless otherwise approved by the Director General under specific conditions.

If records are maintained in an electronically readable format, they must be stored in a way that allows for ready access and conversion into writing. If records originally kept in manual form are later converted to electronic form, the original manual versions must also be preserved. Copies of records are admissible in legal proceedings to the same extent as the originals. This section also applies to any person, other than a taxable person, who acquires imported taxable services in the course of business. Failure to comply with any of these requirements is an offence and may result in a fine of up to RM50,000, imprisonment of up to three years, or both. However, these obligations do not apply to foreign registered persons.

Note that this requirement was available under the repealed Goods and Services Tax Act 2014.

Section 82 imposes a legal duty on all businesses to maintain proper records and, where applicable, issue receipts. Every business must keep accurate and sufficient records for at least seven years from the end of the relevant income year to allow tax authorities to determine taxable income or losses. If gross takings exceed RM150,000 from goods or RM100,000 from services, the business must issue serially numbered printed receipts for each transaction and keep duplicates. Businesses that file late returns must also keep records for seven years from the filing date. 

If a sales recording machine is used, the receipt requirement may be waived, but only if the machine records all transactions accurately. The Director General may set or waive requirements for recordkeeping and receipts and may also request audited financial statements under the Companies Act 2016 if existing records are inadequate. Entries must be made within 60 days of each transaction. Records kept electronically must be readable, accessible, and convertible to written form; if manual records are later digitized, the originals must be preserved.

Moreover, All records that relate to any business in Malaysia shall be kept and retained in Malaysia. This includes records of (a) books of account recording receipts and payments or income and expenditure; (b) invoices, vouchers, receipts and such other documents as in the opinion of the Director General are necessary to verify the entries in any books of account; and (c) any other records as may be specified by the Director General under subsection (3).

Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010;

Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

In Malaysia, there are no explicit, broad-based data localization requirements that mandate all personal data to be stored within the country or to have their own data center in Malaysia. However, specific regulations under certain sectors, like banking, finance, and telecommunications, impose strict conditions on cross-border data transfers, which effectively encourage local processing and can be interpreted as a form of data localization.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017

In the Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector (2017), the conditions for Transfer in Schedule 1 (Rights of Data Subjects), Section 1.1: Any cross-border transfer must ensure compliance with the PDPA’s principles, particularly related to the security of data, and must have written agreements with third-party data processors abroad. Transfer of Personal Data Abroad (Section 4.10)  prohibits the transfer of personal data abroad unless the recipient country ensures adequate protection under similar data protection laws or explicit consent from the data subject is obtained. 

Section 4.10 (Transfer of Personal Data Abroad)  provides that the Act prohibits the transfer of personal data outside of Malaysia unless the transfer is to a country with sufficient data protection laws, as specified by the Minister in a Government Gazette, which will be based on the Commissioner’s recommendation to the Minister.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017
- Transfer of Personal Data Abroad (Section 4.7): Licensees may not transfer personal data abroad unless the receiving country provides an adequate level of protection, or specific consent is obtained from the data subject.
- Third-Party Service Providers (Section 3.2(ii)): If personal data is transferred to third parties outside Malaysia (e.g., for marketing or debt collection), the service provider must comply with the same standards as the local data user, ensuring equivalent data protection abroad

Malaysia has a comprehensive data protection framework established through the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010, which applies to the processing of personal data in commercial transactions. The PDPA regulates how personal data can be collected, used, and disclosed, and it imposes obligations on data users (companies and organizations) to protect the personal data of individuals.

Moreover, the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments to the PDPA further enhance this framework by introducing mandatory data breach notifications, extending data processor obligations, and increasing penalties for non-compliance, aligning Malaysia’s privacy regulations more closely with international standards like the EU’s GDPR. Specifically, data Breach Notification (Section 12b) requires data controllers (including those in banking and communications sectors) to notify the Personal Data Protection Commissioner and affected data subjects in the event of a data breach, especially if the breach poses significant harm. Moreover, penalties (Amendment of Section 5) prescribes that the penalties for non-compliance with the Security Principle or data breach requirements have been increased to 1 million ringgit or imprisonment of up to three years, or both.

In addition to the PDPA, sector-specific codes of practice also exist for industries like banking, finance, and communications, but these operate within the comprehensive legal framework provided by the PDPA.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017

Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector 2017

- Article 3.1: Data Collection and Use: Banks must collect personal data only for purposes directly related to their operational functions, such as account management, financial transactions, and compliance with regulatory requirements. Explicit consent from the customer is mandatory before data collection.
- Article 4.1: Security of Personal Data. Banking institutions must implement technical safeguards, such as encryption and firewalls, and organizational measures, including restricted access, to ensure data protection. Regular audits and security reviews are required to address evolving threats.
- Article 5.1: Retention and Disposal of Data. Personal data must not be retained longer than necessary. The retention period should align with legal or regulatory requirements (e.g., Anti-Money Laundering laws). Secure deletion and destruction processes must be in place when data is no longer needed.
- Article 6.2: Data Subject Access and Correction
Customers have the right to:
	• Request access to their data.
	• Request corrections to inaccuracies in their data.
	• Banks must respond within a reasonable timeframe and may charge reasonable fees for processing such requests.
- Article 7.2: Sharing Data with Third Parties. Personal data may only be disclosed to third parties (e.g., credit reporting agencies, payment processors) with the customer’s consent or as required by law. Agreements with third parties must ensure compliance with equivalent data protection standards.
- Article 8.3: Data Breach Reporting. Banks are required to notify the relevant authorities and affected individuals in the event of a significant data breach impacting personal data.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) 2017

- Article 3.2: Notice and Choice Principle - Licensees must provide clear and accessible notices to individuals regarding the purpose of data collection, retention periods, and customer rights. Individuals must be given the choice to consent to or refuse data collection.
- Article 4.1: Limitation of Use - Personal data can only be processed for purposes that the individual has been explicitly informed of during collection, such as service provision, billing, and fraud prevention.
- Article 5.1: Security Measures -Communication service providers must ensure the confidentiality of customers’ data through the implementation of cybersecurity protocols. Measures such as encryption, user authentication, and intrusion detection systems are mandated.
- Article 6.3: Data Retention - Personal data must be stored only for as long as required for operational, billing, or legal purposes. After the purpose is fulfilled, data must be permanently deleted or anonymized to prevent misuse.
- Article 7.2: Data Access and Accuracy
Customers have the right to:
	• Request access to their personal data stored by the licensee.
	• Rectify incorrect or outdated information.
	• Licensees must facilitate these requests promptly, barring legal exceptions.
- Article 8.1: Domestic Data Transfers - Personal data shared with third parties within Malaysia must adhere to the same security and privacy standards as required by the PDPA. Agreements with third parties (e.g., contractors, service providers) must enforce compliance with this code.
- Article 9.1: Cross-Border Data Restrictions. Although primarily related to international transfers, this article emphasizes that all personal data must be processed domestically unless explicit customer consent is obtained for cross-border processing.
- Article 10.3: Breach Notification. Licensees must promptly notify affected customers and relevant regulators of any data breach compromising personal information.

Computer Crimes Act (Act 563) 1997;

Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024

Malaysia previous cybersecurity law was based on Computer Crimes Act (Act 563) 1997, it criminalizes various cybercrimes, such as unauthorized access to computers, modification of computer content, and other malicious cyber activities. However, it mainly addresses cyber offenses rather than a broader cybersecurity framework. The Cyber Security Act 2024 now provides a comprehensive framework to address national cybersecurity threats and regulate service providers.

The new Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 enhances Malaysia’s national cybersecurity efforts by:
	1. Establishing a National Cyber Security Committee, responsible for overseeing cybersecurity policies and national coordination (Part III - Duties and Powers of Chief Executive).
	2. Defining roles for critical information infrastructure entities and mandating them to comply with cybersecurity risk assessments, audits, and notifications of cybersecurity incidents. Sections 15-24 cover the responsibilities and duties of entities designated as national critical information infrastructure.
	3. Regulating cybersecurity service providers through a licensing regime to ensure that those offering services like managed security operation center monitoring and penetration testing adhere to national standards. Sections 27-34 focus on the licensing of cybersecurity service providers. 
	4. Strengthening enforcement through powers of investigation, search, and seizure, allowing the government to respond effectively to cybersecurity threats (Sections 36-52), thus, enabling the enforcement of cybersecurity regulations and the management of cyber incidents.
This new law positions Malaysia to better handle cyber risks and threats to its critical infrastructures.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

The Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) 2017 also highlights the retention principle, specifying that personal data should be kept only as long as necessary for its intended purpose (Section 5.5). After the purpose has been fulfilled, data must be securely destroyed or deleted (Section 5.0).
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017

In the Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for the Banking and Financial Sector (2017), Section 3.5 prescribes retention principle. Personal data should not be kept longer than necessary for the purpose it was collected. The Act also provides that upon the purpose being fulfilled, Data Users are required to permanently destroy/delete the personal data. This requirement applies to both physical and electronic copies of documents containing personal data.

According to the Bank DP Guide, personal data in the banking sector may be retained for a period of seven years from the date of closure or termination of accounts or facilities Section (Section 3.5.4 and 3.5.5). In certain circumstances, personal data may be retained beyond seven years if required by law or regulatory bodies, such as Bank Negara Malaysia. Upon the fulfillment of the purpose for which personal data was collected, the data must be permanently deleted or destroyed as per Section 3.5.9.

Section 24 of the Service Tax Act 2018 (Act 807) sets out the obligations for recordkeeping by taxable persons. Every taxable person must keep complete, accurate, and up-to-date records of all transactions that affect or may affect their liability to service tax. This includes records of all taxable services provided or received (such as invoices, receipts, debit and credit notes), records of imported taxable services, and any other records as may be determined by the Director General. These records must be preserved for seven years from the most recent date to which they relate, must be kept in either the national language or English, and must be retained in Malaysia unless otherwise approved by the Director General under specific conditions.

If records are maintained in an electronically readable format, they must be stored in a way that allows for ready access and conversion into writing. If records originally kept in manual form are later converted to electronic form, the original manual versions must also be preserved. Copies of records are admissible in legal proceedings to the same extent as the originals. This section also applies to any person, other than a taxable person, who acquires imported taxable services in the course of business. Failure to comply with any of these requirements is an offence and may result in a fine of up to RM50,000, imprisonment of up to three years, or both. However, these obligations do not apply to foreign registered persons.

Note that this requirement was available under the repealed Goods and Services Tax Act 2014.

Section 82 imposes a legal duty on all businesses to maintain proper records and, where applicable, issue receipts. Every business must keep accurate and sufficient records for at least seven years from the end of the relevant income year to allow tax authorities to determine taxable income or losses. If gross takings exceed RM150,000 from goods or RM100,000 from services, the business must issue serially numbered printed receipts for each transaction and keep duplicates. Businesses that file late returns must also keep records for seven years from the filing date. 

If a sales recording machine is used, the receipt requirement may be waived, but only if the machine records all transactions accurately. The Director General may set or waive requirements for recordkeeping and receipts and may also request audited financial statements under the Companies Act 2016 if existing records are inadequate. Entries must be made within 60 days of each transaction. Records kept electronically must be readable, accessible, and convertible to written form; if manual records are later digitized, the originals must be preserved.

Moreover, All records that relate to any business in Malaysia shall be kept and retained in Malaysia. This includes records of (a) books of account recording receipts and payments or income and expenditure; (b) invoices, vouchers, receipts and such other documents as in the opinion of the Director General are necessary to verify the entries in any books of account; and (c) any other records as may be specified by the Director General under subsection (3).

The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments to the Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 (PDPA) introduced new requirements, including the appointment of Data Protection Officers (DPOs) and the implementation of Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs), marking significant changes compared to the original PDPA 2010. Section 12a (1) of the 2024 PDPA amendment mandates that a data controller must appoint one or more Data Protection Officers (DPOs). The DPO is accountable for ensuring compliance with the PDPA, which applies horizontally across all sectors. This is a significant change from the 2010 version, where no explicit requirement for a DPO was mandated.

The 2024 amendments also introduced a requirement for DPIAs in specific cases where data processing activities pose a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. This is especially relevant for large-scale processing of sensitive personal data or new technologies. Similar to the DPO requirement, the DPIA requirement applies horizontally across all sectors when high-risk processing is involved. Another significant change from the 2010 PDPA, as organizations are now required to assess and mitigate risks through formal DPIA procedures.

From the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Bill 2024 (Act A1727) amendments, there is no explicit provision that allows the government to access personal data without a court order. 

The Personal Data Protection Act (Act 709) 2010 PDPA Section 45(2)(a)(i) exempts personal data from the consent requirement if it is processed for the prevention or detection of crime, or for the purposes of investigations. And Section 121 grants authorized officers the power to request the production of documents or data (including computerized data) to ensure compliance with the PDPA. An authorized officer shall have the powers of a police officer of whatever rank as provided for under the Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593)  in relation to investigation, and such powers shall be in addition to and not in derogation of, the powers provided for under this Act (Section 112 Power of investigation). A Magistrate may issue a warrant for an officer to search premises and seize evidence related to an offense, with or without assistance and by force if needed (Section 113 Search and seizure with warrant). However, where delay could harm the investigation, an officer may search without a warrant using the same powers (Section 114 Search and seizure without warrant). 

These specific sectoral guidelines indicate that while court orders are generally required for accessing personal data, exceptions exist, particularly for criminal investigations and regulatory functions, where data can be accessed without consent or court intervention.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice for Licensees Under the Communications and Multimedia (Act 1998) of 2017

As part of the broader legal and regulatory framework, law enforcement agencies and investigative authorities can request access to personal data under certain circumstances, as outlined in related sector-specific guidelines and laws such as the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 (Section 7.4) applies when personal data is processed for the apprehension or prosecution of offenders, or when required for the assessment or collection of any tax or duty.
Personal Data Protection Code of Practice For Banking Sector And Financial Institutions 2017

The Banking and Financial Sector guidelines (Section 3.7.4) includes provisions for processing data for tax assessment or legal obligations, allowing such access without prior notice to the individual.

Section 46 of the Cyber Security Act (Act 854) 2024 concerning access to computerized data allows an authorized officer conducting a search under this Act to be given access to computerized data stored in computers or otherwise.The authorized officer means any policy officer of whatever rank or any public officer authorized by the Minister in writing (Section 4).  

An authorized officer shall have the powers of a police officer of whatever rank as provided for under the Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593)  in relation to investigation, and such powers shall be in addition to and not in derogation of, the powers provided for under this Act (Section 38 Power of investigation). A Magistrate may issue a warrant for an officer to search premises and seize evidence related to an offense, with or without assistance and by force if needed (Section 39 Search and seizure with warrant). However, where delay could harm the investigation, an officer may search without a warrant using the same powers (Section 40 Search and seizure without warrant).

Under Section 6(3) of the Security Offenses (Special Measures) Act (Act 747) 2012 (Power to intercept communication), a police officer with the rank of superintendent of police or above may intercept communications without the authorization of the public prosecutor in urgent and sudden cases where immediate action is required leaving no moment of deliberation. 

Specifically, “communication” means a communication received or transmitted by post or a telegraphic, telephonic or other communication received or transmitted by electricity, magnetism or other means; “communications service provider” means a person who provides services for the transmission or reception of communications (Section 6(7)).

The Criminal Procedure Code (Act 593) 2018 Section 116B (Access to computerized data) of the Criminal Procedure Code provides that a police officer conducting a search under the Code must be given access to computerised data whether stored in a computer or otherwise. For the purpose of this section, 'access' includes being provided with the necessary password, encryption code, decryption code, software, or hardware and any other means required to enable comprehension of the computerised data. It is not clear whether a court order is needed to access the information. 

Additionally, Section 116C (Interception of communication) provides that the public prosecutor has the authority to authorize police officer to intercept, detain and open postal articles. This also includes intercept messages or conversations transmitted by any communication, and install devices on premises for intercepting communications. The Court shall take cognizance of any authorization by the Public Prosecutor under this section.

The My Health Records Act 2012 (Section 77) mandates that such government medical records must not be held or processed outside national borders, ensuring data security and sovereignty. Exceptions apply only if the records do not include personal or identifying information related to healthcare recipients or participants in the system.

The My Health Records Act 2012 (Section 77) mandates that such government medical records must be stored and processed within Australia, ensuring data security and sovereignty. Exceptions apply only if the records do not include personal or identifying information related to healthcare recipients or participants in the system.

Under Australian Privacy Principle 8 (APP 8) of the Privacy Act 1988, cross-border disclosure of personal information is allowed in certain conditions, including when the recipient country has similar privacy protections to Australia, when the individual gives consent, or when required by law. Different rules apply for government data, including restrictions to stay within Australia.

The Privacy Act 1988 is the primary federal legislation that governs protection of personal information and applies horizontally. The Act includes thirteen Privacy Principles, which set out standards, rights and obligations for the handling, holding, use, accessing and correction of personal information (including sensitive information. The Privacy Act's amendment in 2018 introduced a mandatory notification scheme for entities in responding to data breaches. The Notifiable Data Breach Scheme (NDB scheme) introduced an obligation to notify individuals whose personal information is involved in a data breach that is likely to result in serious harm. The NDB scheme applies to all agencies and organisations with existing personal information security obligations under the Australian Privacy Act. The Privacy Act's amendment in 2023 increases the maximum penalties for serious or repeated privacy breaches and provides the Australian Information Commissioner with greater powers to resolve privacy breaches and quickly share information about data breaches.

Australia's Cyber Security is guided through the 2023-2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy, which includes a roadmap for new legislation and standards. 

Until the new legislation under the strategy is passed, Cybersecurity is controlled through a mixture of laws including the Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018, Privacy Act 1988 and the Criminal Code Act 1995. These laws contain cybersecurity-related provisions dispersed across sectoral and thematic statutes, addressing critical infrastructure protection, data security, and cybercrime respectively, but they do not constitute a single, coherent, and horizontally applicable cybersecurity-specific legal framework.

The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 requires a telecommunication service provider to keep specific telecommunications data relating to the services it offers for two years (187C). The dataset to be kept includes: the subscriber and the accounts of telecommunications devices, the source of a communication, the destination of a communication, the date, time and duration of a communication, the type of a communication, and the location of equipment or a line used (187AA). This retention scheme was inserted into the Act by the Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Amendment (Data Retention) Act 2015.

A Privacy Impact Assessment ('PIA') is contemplated by Australian privacy law but, apart from government agencies, is not mandated. However, arguably a PIA is, if not required, highly recommended to fulfil one's obligations under APP 1.2. The guidance and recommendations of the OAIC are that a PIA should be used for any new, changed/varied or altered process, method, or technology used that processes any personal information. In addition, the OAIC may, in writing, direct an agency to conduct a PIA, within a specified period if an agency proposes to engage in an activity or function involving the handling of personal information about individuals, and the OAIC considers that the activity or function might have a significant impact on the privacy of individual (Section 33D(1) of the Privacy Act).

On 1 April 2022, the Data Availability and Transparency Act 2022 commenced, creating a new scheme for sharing Commonwealth Government data. Under the Act, authorised Commonwealth bodies can provide controlled access to public sector data to accredited users, being accredited state and federal government entities and public Australian universities, for specific purposes in the public interest. Specifically, under Article 104, if the Commissioner reasonably believes that a person has information or a document relevant to an investigation under section 101, or to any other of the Commissioner’s regulatory functions set out in section 45, the Commissioner may, by written notice given to the person, require the person to give the information, or produce the document, to the Commissioner within the period specified in the notice.

The Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979 allows law enforcement to intercept user communications, even without a warrant in emergencies or for capability testing. It details various warrants like interception (Section 10) and stored communications (Section 110). The Telecommunications and Other Legislation Amendment (Assistance and Access) Act 2018 provides industry assistance powers, ensuring cooperation with executing these warrants. However, Section 317ZH states these powers are void if they'd require a warrant under other mentioned acts. The Telecommunications Legislation Amendment (International Production Orders) Act 2020 permits the government to retrieve encrypted data from foreign companies. The Telecommunications Regulations 2021 enables cooperation between telecommunications providers, financial institutions and state agencies to reduce cyber risks, including via personal information sharing.

The Telecommunications Act 1997, as amended in 2018, provides for three types of requests or notices that the government and certain security and law enforcement agencies can issue to communications providers

The Australian Security Intelligence Act 1979 includes a computer access warrant (Section 25A) permitting the use of telecom facilities to access relevant data

The Surveillance Legislation Amendment (Identify and Disrupt) Act extends the nation's surveillance capabilities, to disrupt data (Section 6), takeover data (Schedule 3) and monitor network activity (Schedule 2).

The Data Availability and Transparency Bill allows governmental authorities to distribute personal data across its agencies.
